From here, we see that Islam did not forbid taking gods besides Allah and worshipping them except after it explained to people that they are created beings and vassals like themselves, and that might and power belong to Allah entirely. The Exalted said: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ عِبَادٌ أَمْثَالُكُمْ** — "Indeed those whom you invoke besides Allah are creatures like yourselves" (al-A‘raf: 194); and He said: **وَالَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ نَصْرَكُمْ وَلَا أَنْفُسَهُمْ يَنْصُرُونَ وَإِنْ تَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَى الْهُدَىٰ لَا يَسْمَعُوا ۖ وَتَرَاهُمْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَيْكَ وَهُمْ لَا يُبْصِرُونَ** — "And those whom you invoke besides Him cannot help you, nor can they help themselves. If you invite them to guidance, they do not hear. You see them looking at you, but they do not see" (al-A‘raf: 197-198); and the Exalted said: **قُلْ يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ تَعَالَوْا إِلَىٰ كَلِمَةٍ سَوَاءٍ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَكُمْ أَلَّا نَعْبُدَ إِلَّا اللَّهَ وَلَا نُشْرِكَ بِهِ شَيْئًا وَلَا يَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُنَا بَعْضًا أَرْبَابًا مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ ۚ فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَقُولُوا اشْهَدُوا بِأَنَّا مُسْلِمُونَ** — "Say, ‘O People of the Book! Come to a common word between us and you: that we will worship no one but Allah, that we will not ascribe any partner to Him, and that none of us will take others as lords besides Allah.’ But if they turn away, say, ‘Be witnesses that we are Muslims’" (Al ‘Imran: 64). He concluded the verse with the mention of submission (التسليم) to Allah after calling them to abandon the worship of others besides Allah from the gods and to reject submission to other creatures similar to them. And the Exalted said: **أَنَّ الْقُوَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا** — "that power belongs entirely to Allah" (al-Baqarah: 165); and He said: **فَإِنَّ الْعِزَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا** — "for all might belongs to Allah" (an-Nisa: 139); and He said: **مَا لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَلَا شَفِيعٍ** — "You have besides Him no guardian or intercessor" (as-Sajdah: 4); among other verses.

So there is nothing with anyone besides Him, the Exalted, that calls for submission to them. Thus, submission to anyone besides Him is not permissible unless it leads back to submission to Allah, and their honoring, veneration, or guardianship returns to His side. The Exalted said: **الَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ الرَّسُولَ النَّبِيَّ الْأُمِّيَّ** —until He said— **فَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِهِ وَعَزَّرُوهُ وَنَصَرُوهُ وَاتَّبَعُوا النُّورَ الَّذِي أُنْزِلَ مَعَهُ ۙ أُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ** — "those who follow the Apostle, the untaught prophet... those who believe in him, honour him, and help him and follow the light that has been sent down with him, they are the felicitous" (al-A‘raf: 157); and He said: **إِنَّمَا وَلِيُّكُمُ اللَّهُ وَرَسُولُهُ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** —until His saying— **وَهُمْ رَاكِعُونَ** — "Your guardian is only Allah, His Apostle, and the faithful... while they bow down" (al-Ma'idah: 55); and He said: **وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَالْمُؤْمِنَاتُ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِيَاءُ بَعْضٍ ۚ يَأْمُرُونَ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ** — "But the faithful, men and women, are comrades of one another: they bid what is right and forbid what is wrong" (at-Tawbah: 71); and He said: **وَمَنْ يُعَظِّمْ شَعَائِرَ اللَّهِ فَإِنَّهَا مِنْ تَقْوَى الْقُلُوبِ** — "and whoever venerates the sacraments of Allah—indeed that comes from Godwariness of hearts" (al-Hajj: 32). So there is no submission in Islam to anyone besides Allah except that which returns to Him, the Exalted, and is intended for Him.

**3 – How did Idolatry originate?** And with what did it begin? It became clear in the previous section that man is in a position prone to corporealizing (تجسيم) spiritual matters and casting the non-sensible into the mold of the sensible through representation and imagery. He is, nonetheless, naturally disposed to submit before any superior, coercive power and to pay heed to its affair.

Therefore, the spirit of polytheism (الشرك) and idolatry (الوثنية) has permeated human society in a way that is almost impossible to guard against or avoid, even in modern advanced societies and even in societies built on the basis of rejecting religion. You see in them monuments and statues of men, their veneration and respect, and the extent of submission to them that represents to you the idolatry of the early eras and the early human. Furthermore, there are hundreds of millions of idolaters on the face of the earth today, residing in its east and west.

From here, it is supported by consideration that idolatry began among people by taking statues of great men and erecting their idols, especially after death, so that there would be a remembrance of them. Narrations from the Imams of the *Ahl al-Bayt* have been transmitted supporting this. In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, as a *mudmar* narration, and in *‘Ilal al-Shara'i‘*, as a *musnad* narration from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَقَالُوا لَا تَذَرُنَّ آلِهَتَكُمْ** (Nuh: 23), he said: "They used to worship Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, then they died—their people cried out and that was hard upon them—so Iblis, may Allah curse him, came to them and said: 'I shall make for you idols in their images—so you may look at them and find comfort in them and worship Allah.' So he prepared for them idols in their likeness, and they used to worship Allah, the Mighty and Majestic—while looking at those idols. When winter and rains came to them, they brought the idols into the houses.

They continued to worship Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, until that generation perished—and their children grew up and said: 'Our fathers used to worship these'—so they worshipped them besides Allah, the Mighty and Majestic. That is the saying of Allah, the Blessed and Exalted: **وَلَا تَذَرُنَّ وَدًّا وَلَا سُوَاعًا**."

The master of the house in ancient Rome and Greece—as history mentions—used to be worshipped in his house. When he died, an idol was made for him which the people of his house worshipped. Many kings and great men were worshipped among their people. The Holy Quran mentioned among them Nimrod, the king contemporary to Ibrahim (عليه السلام) who argued with him about his Lord, and the Pharaoh of Musa.

And here they are found in the houses of idols existing today, as well as among the ancient relics preserved from them: idols of many great men of religion, such as the idol of Buddha and the idols of many Brahmins and others.

Their taking idols of the dead and worshipping them is among the evidences that they believed they were not nullified by death and that their souls remain after it, having the same care and effect as they had during their lives; rather, after death, they are stronger in existence, more effective in will, and more intense in influence because they have been purified from the mixture of matter and saved from corporeal influences and physical passivities. The Pharaoh of Musa used to worship idols of his own while he was a god and worshipped among his people. The Exalted said: **وَقَالَ الْمَلَأُ مِنْ قَوْمِ فِرْعَوْنَ أَتَذَرُ مُوسَىٰ وَقَوْمَهُ لِيُفْسِدُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَيَذَرَكَ وَآلِهَتَكَ** — "The elite of Pharaoh’s people said, ‘Will you leave Moses and his people to cause corruption in the land, and to abandon you and your gods?’" (al-A‘raf: 127).

**4 – Taking Idols for the Lords of Species and Others:** Taking statues of men was what alerted people to taking an idol of God. However, it was not known of them to take a statue of Allah, the Glorified, who is too exalted for any limit to encompass Him or any imagination to reach Him. It seems this is what diverted them from taking an idol of Him; instead, they diverged in that, and each took what concerned him of the aspects of management observed in the world. They sought a means to the worship of Allah by worshipping those whom Allah had entrusted, in their view, with the management of that specific aspect.

So those residing on the seacoasts worshipped the Lord of the Sea to bestow its benefits upon them and to save them from floods and surging; the inhabitants of valleys worshipped the Lord of the Valley; the people of war worshipped the Lord of War, and so on.

They did not wait long before each of them took what he desired of a god in what he imagined of image and form, and from what he chose of metal, wood, stone, or otherwise. It is even narrated that the Banu Hanifa from Yamama took for themselves an idol made of dried curd (أقط); then a drought struck them and hunger encompassed them, so they fell upon it and ate it.

A man, if he found a beautiful tree or a beautiful stone and desired it, would worship it. They used to slaughter sheep or sacrifice camels and smear it with their blood. If a disease struck their livestock, they would bring them to it and rub them against it. They used to take many trees as lords, seeking blessings from them without touching them by cutting or breaking, drawing near to them with sacrifices, and bringing to them vows and gifts.

This chaos led them to adopt various divergent views regarding idols that can hardly be systematized or enumerated. However, the predominant belief among them was that they took these idols as intercessors (شفعاء) through whom they sought mediation with Allah, the Glorified, to bring them good and repel evil. At times, some of the common folk took them as objects of worship (معبودة) in their own right, independent in their divinity (الألوهية) without being mere intercessors. Occasionally, they would take them as intercessors but prioritize or prefer them over Allah, the Glorified, as the Quran recounts in His saying, the Exalted: **فَما كانَ لِشُرَكائِهِمْ فَلا يَصِلُ إِلَى اللهِ وَما كانَ لِلَّهِ فَهُوَ يَصِلُ إِلى شُرَكائِهِمْ** — "So what is for their partners does not reach Allah, and what is for Allah reaches their partners" (6:136).

Some of them worshipped the angels (الملائكة), others worshipped the jinn (الجن), and some worshipped the fixed stars (الكواكب الثابتة) such as Sirius (الشعرى). One group took some of the planets (السيارات) as gods—all of which has been pointed out in the Divine Book—all out of desire for their perceived benefit or fear of their harm.

Rarely was a god taken besides Allah without an idol (صنم) being fashioned for it to be the focus of worship. Rather, whenever they took something as a mediating god, they would craft an idol for it from wood, stone, or metal, representing it in the form of life they imagined for it. Thus, they would shape it in the form of a human or an animal, even if the entity represented by the idol did not possess that form, such as the fixed stars, the planets, or the gods of knowledge, love, sustenance, war, and the like.

The rationale for adopting idols of these "partners" was their claim that the Deity—due to His transcendence beyond sensible forms, like the lords of the species (أرباب الأنواع) and other non-material deities, or due to His lack of permanence in manifestation, like a star that shifts from rising to setting—is difficult to turn toward whenever one wishes. Therefore, they deemed it necessary to adopt an idol that represents Him in His attributes and descriptions, so that one may direct oneself toward Him through its medium whenever desired.

**5 - Sabian Idolatry:**
Idolatry (الوثنية), although it traces back—approximately—to a single origin, which is the adoption of intercessors (الشفعاء) to Allah and the worship of their idols and statues, has perhaps dominated the earth and encompassed the human world repeatedly. This is as the Quran recounts regarding the nations contemporary to Nuh, Ibrahim, and Musa (peace be upon them). However, the divergence among its adherents reached such a level of fragmentation and following of whims and superstitions that categorizing the resulting sects is nearly impossible, as most of them are not built upon established principles (أصول) or organized, consistent rules.

Among those that can be considered a sect close to being organized and coherent are the Sabian (الصابئة), Brahmanical (البرهمية), and Buddhist (البوذية) idolatries.

As for Sabian idolatry (الوثنية الصابئة), it is built upon linking generation and corruption (الكون والفساد) and the events of the terrestrial world to the celestial bodies (الأجرام العلوية) such as the Sun, the Moon, Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn. They believe that these bodies, by virtue of the spiritual entities (الروحانيات) associated with them, are the governors of the observed order. Each governs the events related to it according to what the art of astrology (أحكام النجوم) describes, and cycles and epochs (الأدوار والأكوار) repeat with the repetition of their orbits without ceasing or reaching a final end.

Thus, they are intermediaries (وسائط) between Allah, the Glorified, and this observed world; worshipping them brings man closer to Him, the Exalted. Consequently, it was deemed necessary to adopt idols and statues for them, seeking proximity to them through the worship of those idols and statues.

Historians mention that the one who established its foundation and refined its principles and branches was "Yudhasaf" the astrologer, who appeared in the land of India during the time of Tahmuras, the King of Iran. He called to the Sabian creed, and many followed him. His doctrine spread to the corners of the earth, such as Rome, Greece, Babylon, and elsewhere. Temples (هياكل) and sanctuaries were built for it, containing idols of the stars, and they possessed laws, rituals, slaughters, and offerings (قرابين) overseen by their priests. Sometimes the sacrifice of humans is attributed to them.

These people profess the oneness of Allah in His divinity (ألوهيته) but not in His worship (عبادته). They transcend Him beyond deficiencies and foulness, describing Him through negation (النفي) rather than affirmation (الإثبات), such as saying He is not incapable, not ignorant, does not die, does not wrong, and is not unjust. They call these the "Beautiful Names" (الأسماء الحسنى) metaphorically, not believing in them as literal names. We have previously presented some of their history in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَالَّذِينَ هادُوا وَالنَّصارى وَالصَّابِئِينَ** — "Indeed the faithful, the Jews, the Christians and the Sabaeans..." (2:62) in the first volume of this book.

**6 - Brahmanical Idolatry:**
Brahmanism (البرهمية)—as mentioned—is one of the deep-rooted idolatrous sects, and perhaps the oldest among people, for Indian civilization is among the oldest human civilizations. No precise historical beginning can be established for it, nor for the idolatry of India. However, some historians like al-Mas'udi and others mentioned that "Brahman" was the name of the first king of India who developed its lands, established the foundations of civilization, and spread justice among its people.

Perhaps Brahmanism arose after him in his name, for ancient nations often worshipped their kings and the great figures of their peoples due to their belief that they possessed occult power (سلطة غيبية) and that the Divine (اللاهوت) manifested in them in some way. This is somewhat supported by the fact that the *Vedas* (ويدا)—their sacred book—appears to be a collection of various treatises and essays, each part of which was authored by certain religious men at different times and inherited by those after them. Thus, they were collected and compiled into a book indicating an organized religion. Sanskrit scholars have explicitly stated this, and the necessary consequence is that Brahmanism, like other idolatrous sects, began from common, unrefined ideas and evolved through stages of development until it reached its share of completion.

Al-Bustani mentioned in the *Encyclopedia* a summary as follows:

"Brahm" (with two *fathas* and a *sukun*, or *fatha* on the *ba* and *ha* and *sukun* on the *ra*) is the first and greatest deity among the Indians. To them, he is the origin of all existents, one, unchanging, and unperceivable; eternal (أزلي), absolute (مطلق), preceding every creature. He created the entire world simply by his will, all at once, by his saying: **Om** (أوم), meaning "Be."

The story of Brahm resembles in every respect the story of "Adi Buddha"; the difference lies only in name and attributes. They often make the name Brahm itself a name for the three persons (الأقانيم) that constitute the Indian trinity (ثالوث): "Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva." The worshippers of Brahm are called Brahmanists or Brahmins.

As for Brahma, he is Brahm himself, the deity of the Indians, after he commenced his acts (evidenced by the addition of the *alif* at the end, which is one of their conventions). He is the first person of the Indian trinity; that is, Brahm emanates within himself into three persons each time. The first person through which he appears the first time is Brahma, the second is Vishnu, and the third is Shiva.

When Brahma emanated, he remained for a long time sitting upon a lotus tree called *Kamala* in Hindi and *Padma* in Sanskrit. He looked in every direction, having four heads with eight eyes, but saw nothing but a vast, dark space filled with water. He was terrified by this and could not perceive the secret of his origin, so he remained silent, mute, and immersed in contemplations.

Ages passed in this state until suddenly a voice struck his ears, awakening him from his slumber and instructing him to seek refuge in "Bhagavan," which is a title of Brahm. Then Brahm appeared in the form of a man with a thousand heads. Brahma prostrated to him and began to praise him. Bhagavan’s breast expanded, and he innovated light, dispelled the darkness, and revealed to his servant the state of his being and the beings in the form of dormant germs (جراثيم متخدرة), giving him the power to bring them out of this lethargy.

Brahma remained contemplating this for a hundred divine years—which is equivalent to thirty-six thousand solar years. Then he began the work, first innovating the seven heavens called by them *Swarga* (سورغة) and illuminating them with the bodies called *Devas* (ديقانة). Then he innovated *Mrityuloka* (مريثلوكا), meaning the abode of death, then the earth and its moon, then the seven lower dwellings called *Patala* (بتالة), illuminating them with eight jewels placed on the heads of eight serpents.

Thus, the seven heavens and the seven lower dwellings are the fourteen worlds in Indian mythology.

Then he created seven pairs to assist him in his works, but ten of them refused to help him, namely the *Munis* and the nine *Rishis*, among whom was *Narada*. They restricted themselves to worldly contemplations. He then married his sister *Saraswati* and fathered a hundred children with her. The firstborn was named *Daksha*. Daksha had fifty daughters, thirteen of whom married *Kashyapa*, whom they sometimes call the first Brahman, and he is the one who fathered for Brahma a son named *Marichi*.

One of the aforementioned daughters, named *Aditi*, gave birth to the luminous spirits called *Devas*, who do good and inhabit the heavens. As for her sister *Diti*, she gave birth to a vast multitude of evil spirits called *Daityas* or *Asuras*, who are the inhabitants of darkness and the doers of all evil in the world.

The earth until that time was devoid of inhabitants. Some said that Brahma brought forth from himself *Manu Svayambhuva*, whom others say preceded him and is Brahm himself, the one deity. Then Brahma married him to *Shatarupa* and told them to multiply and increase.

Others said that Brahma fathered four sons: Brahman, Kshatriya, Vaishya, and Shudra. The first came out of his mouth, the second from his right arm, the third from his right thigh, and the fourth from his right foot. Thus, they were four roots for four original castes.

The last three married three women who also came from him: one from his right arm, the second from his left thigh, and the third from his left foot. They were named after their husbands with the addition of the feminine marker *ni*. Brahman also married a wife from his father, but she was from the lineage of the evil *Asuras*. This is what is in the *Vedas* regarding the creation of the world.

Then Brahma, after being the powerful creator god, fell from the rank of Vishnu, the second person, and Shiva, the third person. This was because he became inflated with pride and vanity, thinking himself the peer of the Most High. Thus, he fell into *Naraka*, meaning Hell, and did not obtain forgiveness except on the condition that he incarnate once in each of the four ages. He incarnated the first time in the form of a poetic crow named *Kakabhushundi*; the second time in the form of *Valmiki*, who was first a thief, then a somber, grave, and repentant man, then a famous translator of the *Vedas* and author of the *Ramayana*; the third time in the form of *Vyasa*, the poet and author of the *Mahabharata*, the *Bhagavata*, and several *Puranas*; and the fourth time—which is the current age called *Kali Yuga*—in the form of *Kalidasa*, the great dramatic poet and author of *Shakuntala* and the reviser of Valmiki's works.

Furthermore, Brahma appeared in three states: in the first state, he was the One, the Eternal (الصمد), the Great All, the Most High; in the second state, he appeared emanating from the first, i.e., commencing the work; and in the third state, he appeared incarnated in the form of a human and a sage.

Brahma does not have public worship in India; he has only one temple there. However, the Brahmins make him the object of their worship, calling upon him evening and morning. They throw water three times with the palms of their hands onto the ground and toward the sun, and they renew their worship of him at noon by offering him a flower. In the sanctification of fire, they offer him clarified butter as they offer to the god of fire. This sanctification is more important and sacred than all else. Its name is *Homa* or *Homa-raghib*.

Brahma is represented in the form of a man with a long beard, holding in one hand the chain of beings and in the other the vessel containing the celestial water of life, riding the *Hamsa*, which is the divine bird resembling a stork and an eagle.

As for Brahman, he is the firstborn son of Brahma, whom he brought forth from his mouth as previously mentioned, and he assigned to him the four sacred books called *Vedas*, representing the four words he spoke with his four mouths.

When Brahman wished to marry like his brothers, Brahma said to him: "You were born for study and prayer, so you must distance yourself from physical relations." Brahman was not convinced by his father's words, so Brahma became angry and married him to one of the evil spirits called *Asuras*. From this were born the Brahmins, who are the sacred priests designated for the interpretation of the *Vedas*, and they oversaw all the offerings that the Indians presented to the gods.

Kshatriya, the caste of warriors, was born from the Brahmins; Vaishya, the caste of agriculturists; and Shudra, the caste of slaves. Thus, the Brahmins are of four types." — *End of the summary from al-Bustani's Encyclopedia.*

Others mentioned that Brahmanism is divided into four castes: the Brahmins (the scholars of the creed), the warriors, the farmers, and the merchants. No regard is given to others, such as women and slaves. We have quoted in the supplement to His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا عَلَيْكُمْ أَنْفُسَكُمْ** — "O you who have faith! Take care of your own souls" (5:105) in the sixth volume of this book, in a scholarly discussion regarding the book *Indica* by Abu Rayhan al-Biruni, some of the duties and worships of the Brahmins, as well as from *al-Milal wa al-Nihal* by al-Shahrastani, a portion of the laws of the Sabians.

The Indian idolatrous sects—and the Sabians were like them—are in agreement regarding the doctrine of reincarnation (التناسخ). This is the belief that the worlds are infinite from the perspectives of pre-eternity (الأزل) and post-eternity (الأبد), and each has a fixed term of existence. When its term expires, its form is nullified, and from it another world is born, which lives and then dies, giving rise to a third, and so on. Human souls attached to bodies do not die with the death of their bodies; rather, the death of their bodies is the beginning of a new life for them. They attach to other bodies in which they live a happy life if they acquired spiritual virtues and performed righteous deeds in their previous body, and a wretched life if they were clothed in vices and committed sins—except for those perfect in the knowledge of Brahm (Allah, the Glorified), for they are alive with the eternal life, safe from a second birth, and outside the dominion of reincarnation.

## 7 - Buddhist Idolatry:

Brahmanical idolatry was reformed by Buddhism, attributed to Buddha "Sakyamuni," who died in the year 543 BC according to what is narrated from Ceylonese history. Other dates have been suggested, such that the discrepancy reaches two thousand years. For this reason, it was sometimes thought that he was a mythical figure with no reality. However, recent excavations in modern Gaya and other remains in its vicinity have proven the reality of his existence. Through these, other traces of his life history and the teachings he delivered to his disciples and followers have been uncovered.

Buddha was from a royal house, the son of a king named "Suddhodana." His soul turned away from the world and its desires, and he secluded himself from people in his youth, remaining in some desolate forests for years of his life, devoted to asceticism and spiritual discipline (الارتياض) until his soul was illuminated with knowledge. He came out to the people at the age of thirty-six, as is said, calling them to rid themselves of misery and pain and to achieve the great rest and the eternal, everlasting celestial life. He exhorted them and urged them to hold fast to the hem of his law by adopting noble character, rejecting desires, and avoiding vices.

Buddha—according to what is narrated—used to say of himself without Brahmanical pride: "I am a beggar (١), and there exists only one law for all: severe punishment for criminals and great reward for the righteous. My law is a law of grace for all, and in it, like the sky, there is room for men, women, boys, girls, the rich, and the poor—though it is difficult for the rich to walk its path."

His teaching, according to the Buddhists, was that nature is void (ذات فراغ), that it is an illusory deception, and that nothingness (العدم) exists in every place and every time, and it is full of deceit. This very nothingness removes all barriers between the classes of people, their nationalities, and their worldly conditions, making the lowliest of worms brothers to the Buddhists.

They believe that the last phrase Sakyamuni uttered was: "Every composite is transitory." The ultimate goal for them is the salvation of the soul from all pain and delusion, and that the cycle of reincarnation, which has no end, terminates or is cut off by preventing the soul from being born again. This is achieved by purifying it even from the desire for existence.

These fundamental rules of Buddhism are explicitly found in its oldest teaching included in the *Arya Satyani*, which are four noble truths attributed to Sakyamuni, mentioned in his first sermon delivered in a forest known as the Deer Park near Benares.

Those four truths concern pain, its origin, its annihilation, and the path leading to annihilation. Pain is birth, old age, illness, death, encountering the disliked, parting from the beloved, and inability to achieve what is sought. The causes of pain are psychological and physical desires and whims. The annihilation of all these causes is the third truth. The path of annihilation also has eight parts: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration. This is the form of faith for them, and it has been found engraved on many buildings and recorded in several books.

As for the summary of Buddhist ethics, it is the avoidance of every evil thing, the performance of every righteous deed, and the refinement of the mind.

This is what they have accepted from the teaching of Buddha. Beyond this, the worships, sacrifices, priesthood, philosophy, and mysteries are matters added to it with the passing of days and the lapse of ages, and they include strange sayings and views regarding the creation of the world, its order, and other things.

It is said that Buddha never spoke about God. However, this was not due to an aversion to the Principle of Existence (مبدأ الوجود) nor a denial, but because the man was exerting all his effort in equipping people with asceticism regarding the splendor of the life of this world and turning them away from this deceptive abode.

**8 - Idolatry of the Arabs.**
They were the first whom Islam confronted with the call to monotheism (التوحيد) among the idolaters. Most of the Arabs in the era of ignorance (الجاهلية) were Bedouins, and the urbanized among them, like those in Yemen, were Bedouin in nature. They were governed by mixed and varied customs and manners taken from their powerful neighbors such as the Persians, Romans, Egyptians, Abyssinians, and Indians, including religious customs.

Their ancient ancestors—the original Arabs (العرب العاربة), including 'Ad of Iram and Thamud—were upon the religion of idolatry, as Allah, the Glorified, recounts in His Book regarding the people of Hud and Salih, the companions of Madyan, and the people of Saba in the story of Sulayman and the hoopoe. This continued until Ibrahim (peace be upon him) brought his son Isma'il and his mother Hajar to the land of Mecca, which was a valley without cultivation, inhabited by the tribe of Jurhum. He settled them there, Isma'il (peace be upon him) grew up, the town of Mecca was built, and Ibrahim (peace be upon him) built the Kaaba, the Sacred House, and called the people to his upright religion (دينه الحنيف), which is Islam. He was answered in the Hijaz and its surroundings, and he legislated the Hajj for them, as indicated by the entirety of Allah the Exalted's saying to him in what the Quran recounts: **وَأَذِّنْ فِي النَّاسِ بِالْحَجِّ يَأْتُوكَ رِجالاً وَعَلى كُلِّ ضامِرٍ يَأْتِينَ مِنْ كُلِّ فَجٍّ عَمِيقٍ** — "And proclaim the Hajj to the people: they shall come to you on foot and on every lean camel coming from every distant pathway" (22:27).

Then some of the Arabs became Jews due to the social interaction between them and the Jews residing in the Hijaz, and Christianity seeped into some regions of the Peninsula, and Magianism into others.

Then events occurred between the progeny of Isma'il and Jurhum in Mecca until it led to the victory of the progeny of Isma'il and the expulsion of Jurhum from it, and 'Amr ibn Luhayy took control of Mecca and its surroundings.

He then fell severely ill, and it was said to him: "In al-Balqa' in the land of Sham, there is a hot spring; if you bathe in it, you will be cured." He headed there, bathed, and was cured. He saw people there worshipping idols, so he asked them about them. They said: "These are lords we have taken in the form of celestial temples and human persons; we seek victory through them and are granted victory, and we seek rain through them and are granted rain." This pleased him, so he requested an idol from them, and they gave him Hubal. He returned to Mecca and placed it upon the Kaaba. With him were Isaf and Na'ila, which were two idols in the form of a couple—as in *al-Milal wa al-Nihal*—or two youths—as in other sources. He called the people to the worship of idols and promoted that among his people. Thus, they returned to worshipping them after their Islam. They used to be called *Hunafa'* (حنفاء) because of their following the creed of Ibrahim (peace be upon him), so the name remained with them while the meaning left them, and *Hunafa'* became a name for the idolaters (١) among them.

Among the things that drew them closer to idolatry was that the Noble Kaaba was venerated by Jews, Christians, Magians, and idolaters alike. No traveler would depart from Mecca without carrying with him something of the stones of the Sanctuary (الحرم) out of seeking blessing (تبرك) and longing. Wherever they settled, they would place it and circumambulate it for good fortune and love for the Kaaba and the Sanctuary.

Due to these reasons, idolatry spread among the Arabs, both the original and the Arabized, and none remained among the people of monotheism except individuals who are not mentioned. Among the idols known among them were Hubal, Isaf, and Na'ila—which were brought by 'Amr ibn Luhayy and to which he called the people—and al-Lat, al-'Uzza, Manat, Wadd, Suwa', Yaghuth, Ya'uq, and Nasr. These eight are mentioned in the Quran, and the last five are attributed to the people of Nuh.

It is narrated in *al-Kafi*, with its chain to Abd al-Rahman ibn al-Ashall, the carpet seller, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "Yaghuth was placed opposite the door of the Kaaba, Ya'uq was to the right of the Kaaba, and Nasr was to its left."

In the narration also: "Hubal was on the roof of the Kaaba, and Isaf and Na'ila were on al-Safa and al-Marwa." In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, he said: "Wadd belonged to Kalb, Suwa' to Hudhayl, Yaghuth to Murad, Ya'uq to Hamdan, and Nasr to Husayn."

In the idolatry they practiced, there were traces of Sabian idolatry, such as the ritual bath (الغسل) from major ritual impurity (الجنابة) and other things.

In it were also traces of Brahmanism, such as the belief in the lunar mansions (الأنواء) and the belief in time (الدهر), as previously mentioned regarding the idolatry of Buddha. The Exalted said: **وَقالُوا ما هيَ إِلَّا حَياتُنَا الدُّنْيا نَمُوتُ وَنَحْيا وَما يُهْلِكُنا إِلَّا الدَّهْرُ** — "They say, ‘There is nothing but the life of this world: we live and we die, and nothing destroys us but time’" (45:24), although some have mentioned that this is the saying of the materialists who deny the existence of the Creator.

In it was something of the upright religion (الدين الحنيف), which is the Islam of Ibrahim (peace be upon him), such as circumcision (الختنة) and Hajj, except that they mixed it with idolatrous customs like touching the idols around the Kaaba, circumambulating naked, and the *talbiya* with their saying: "At Your service, O Allah, at Your service; You have no partner, except a partner who is Yours; You possess him and what he possesses."

They had other matters they invented themselves, such as the belief in the *bahira*, *sa'iba*, *wasila*, and *ham*, and the belief in the *sada* and *hama*, and the *ansab* (altars) and *azlam* (divining arrows), and other matters mentioned in histories. The explanation of the *bahira*, *sa'iba*, *wasila*, and *ham* has preceded in Surah al-Ma'ida under the commentary of verse 103, as well as the mention of *azlam* and *ansab* under verse 3 and verse 90.

**9 - Islam's Defense of Monotheism and its Confrontation with Idolatry.**
The Divine Call has never ceased to dispute with idolatry, resist it, and summon to monotheism (التوحيد), as Allah mentioned in His Book in what He recounts of the call of the prophets and messengers such as Nuh, Hud, Salih, Ibrahim, Shu'ayb, and Musa (peace be upon them), and this is pointed out in the stories of 'Isa, Lut, and Yunus (peace be upon them).

The matter has been summarized in His saying, the Exalted: **وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا نُوحِي إِلَيْهِ أَنَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنَا فَاعْبُدُونِ** — "We did not send any apostle before you but that We revealed to him that ‘There is no god except Me; so worship Me’" (21:25).

The Prophet Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) began his public call by inviting the idolaters of his people to monotheism with wisdom, goodly exhortation, and disputation in the best manner. They answered him only with mockery, harm, and the persecution of those among them who believed, torturing them with the severest punishment until a group of Muslims was forced to leave Mecca and migrate to Abyssinia. Then they plotted to kill him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), so he migrated to Medina, and several of the believers migrated to it after him.

It was not long before they engaged him in combat, fighting him at Badr, Uhud, and al-Khandaq, and in many other battles until Allah the Exalted granted him victory over them with the Conquest of Mecca. He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) then purified the House and the Sanctuary of their idols and broke the idols stationed around the Noble Kaaba. Hubal was stationed on the roof of the Kaaba, so he had 'Ali (عليه السلام) climb up to it and throw it to the ground. It was—according to what is said—their greatest idol, and it was buried—according to what they mentioned—at the threshold of the mosque's door.

Islam is intensely concerned with severing the roots of idolatry, clearing hearts of the thoughts that invite to it, and diverting souls even from hovering around it or overlooking it. This is observed in what it summons to of original knowledge (المعارف الأصلية), noble character, and legal rulings. Thus, you see it considering the true belief to be that there is no god but Allah; to Him belong the Beautiful Names; He possesses everything; He has the original existence (الوجود الأصيل) that is independent in its essence; He is the Self-Sufficient (الغني) beyond all the worlds; everything other than Him begins from Him and returns to Him, and is in need of Him in all the affairs of its essence, both in its origination and its survival. Therefore, whoever attributes to anything any degree of independence in relation to Him, the Exalted—not in relation to others—in any aspect of its essence, attributes, or acts, is a polytheist (مشرك) accordingly.

And you see it commanding reliance (التوكل) upon Allah, trust in Allah, entering under the guardianship (ولاية) of Allah, love for the sake of Allah, hatred for the sake of Allah, and sincerity of action for Allah. It forbids depending on other than Allah, leaning toward other than Him, finding tranquility in apparent causes, hoping in those besides Him, vanity (العجب), pride (الكبر), and other things that necessitate granting independence to other than Him and associating partners with Him.

And you see it forbidding prostration (السجدة) to other than Him, the Exalted; forbidding the adoption of statues that cast shadows and the depiction of animate beings; forbidding the obedience of other than Allah and listening to them in what they command and forbid, except what returns to the obedience of Allah, such as the obedience of the prophets and the leaders of the religion (أئمة الدين); and forbidding innovation (البدعة) and following it, and following the footsteps of Satan.

The narrations (الأخبار) transmitted from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) are concurrent in that polytheism (الشرك) is divided into manifest (جلي) and hidden (خفي), and that polytheism possesses many levels from which none are safe except the sincere (المخلصون), and that it is more hidden than the creeping of an ant on a smooth stone in a dark night. It is narrated in *al-Kafi* from al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) regarding His saying, the Exalted: **يَوْمَ لا يَنْفَعُ مالٌ وَلا بَنُونَ ـ إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى اللهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ** — "The day when neither wealth nor children will avail, except him who comes to Allah with a sound heart" (26:88-89): "The sound heart (القلب السليم) is the one that meets its Lord while there is no one in it besides Him." He said: "Every heart in which there is polytheism or doubt is fallen; they only intended by asceticism in the world to free their hearts for the Hereafter."

It is also reported: "Worshipping Him, the Exalted, out of desire for Paradise is the worship of hirelings; worshipping Him out of fear of the Fire is the worship of slaves; and the true worship is to worship Him, the Exalted, out of love for Him—and that is the worship of the noble. This is a hidden station—none touch it except the purified." Several of these narrations have preceded in some of the previous discussions of the book.

**10 - Building the Prophet's Conduct upon Monotheism and the Negation of Partners:**
The Exalted summarized his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) conduct, which He commanded him to adopt and proceed with in human society, in His saying: **قُلْ يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ تَعالَوْا إِلى كَلِمَةٍ سَواءٍ بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَكُمْ أَلَّا نَعْبُدَ إِلَّا اللهَ وَلا نُشْرِكَ بِهِ شَيْئاً وَلا يَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُنا بَعْضاً أَرْباباً مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَقُولُوا اشْهَدُوا بِأَنَّا مُسْلِمُونَ** — "Say, ‘O People of the Book! Come to a common word between us and you: that we will worship no one but Allah, that we will not ascribe any partner to Him, and that some of us will not take others as lords besides Allah.’ But if they turn away, say, ‘Be witnesses that we are Muslims’" (3:64). And the Exalted said, pointing to the idolatrous beliefs that entered their religion: **قُلْ يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ لا تَغْلُوا فِي دِينِكُمْ غَيْرَ الْحَقِّ وَلا تَتَّبِعُوا أَهْواءَ قَوْمٍ قَدْ ضَلُّوا مِنْ قَبْلُ وَأَضَلُّوا كَثِيراً وَضَلُّوا عَنْ سَواءِ السَّبِيلِ** — "Say, ‘O People of the Book! Do not exceed the bounds in your religion unduly, and do not follow the desires of a people who went astray before, and led many astray, and strayed from the right way’" (5:77).

He also said, censuring the People of the Book: **اتَّخَذُوا أَحْبارَهُمْ وَرُهْبانَهُمْ أَرْباباً مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ وَالْمَسِيحَ ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ وَما أُمِرُوا إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُوا إِلهاً واحِداً لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ سُبْحانَهُ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ** — "They have taken their scribes and their monks as lords besides Allah, and also the Messiah, son of Mary; though they were not commanded except to worship the One God; there is no god except Him; immaculate is He of any partners they may ascribe [to Him]" (9:31).

He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) had equalized between people in the implementation of laws and punishments (الحدود) and brought the classes of society closer together, such as the ruler and the ruled, the leader and the led, the servant and the served, the rich and the poor, the man and the woman, the noble and the lowly. There is no honor, pride, or authority for anyone over another except the honor of God-consciousness (التقوى), and the reckoning is to Allah and the judgment is His.

He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to distribute equally and forbade the strong from displaying their strength in a way that would affect or break the heart of the weak and humble, such as the rich displaying their adornment over the poor and destitute, or rulers and leaders displaying their might over the subjects.

He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) lived as one of the people, not distinguishing himself from them in food, drink, clothing, seating, gait, or anything else. The summaries of his conduct have preceded at the end of the sixth volume of this book.

## (Another Discourse Appended to the Previous Discourse)

In which we weigh the teaching of the Noble Quran by comparing it to the teachings of the *Vedas*, the *Avesta*, the *Torah*, and the *Gospel* in a general and holistic manner across chapters. This is a noble analytical discussion.

## 1 - Reincarnation among the Idolaters:

Among the primary principles (الأصول الأولية) upon which Brahmanism—and similarly Buddhism and Sabianism—is built is reincarnation (التناسخ). This is the belief that the world is constantly governed by generation and corruption (الكون والفساد). Thus, this world observed by us, as well as the parts within it, is formed from a world like it preceding it, and so on infinitely. This world will perish just as its parts continue to perish, and from it another world will be formed, and so on infinitely. Man lives in each of these worlds according to what he acquired in a world preceding it. Whoever performs righteous deeds and acquires a good disposition (ملكة حسنة), his soul, after parting from the body at death, will attach to a happy body and live in happiness, which is his reward. Whoever clings to the earth and follows his whims will live after death in a wretched body and endure types of torment therein—except for the one who knows Brahm and unites with him, for he is saved from the second birth and returns to an eternal, everlasting essence (ذاتا أزلية أبدية) that is the very essence of splendor, joy, life, power, and knowledge, to which there is no path for annihilation or nullification.

Therefore, it was a religious duty for man to believe in Brahm (who is Allah, the origin of everything), to seek proximity to him through offerings and worships, and to be adorned with noble character and righteous deeds. If his soul turns away from the world and he adopts noble character, performs righteous deeds, and knows Brahm through the knowledge of his own self, he becomes a "Brahmana" and unites with Brahm, becoming him. This is the great happiness and pure life. Otherwise, let him believe in Brahm and perform righteous deeds so that he may be happy in his next life, which is his hereafter.

However, since Brahm is an absolute essence (ذات مطلقة) encompassing everything and not encompassed by anything, he was too high and exalted for man to know him except through a kind of negation of deficiencies (نفي النقائص) or to reach him through worship or offering. Therefore, it is incumbent upon us to seek proximity through worship to his favorites and the powerful among his creation so that they may be intercessors (شفعاء) for us with him. These are the gods who are worshipped besides Allah through the worship of their idols. Despite their numbers, they are either from the angels, the jinn, or the souls of the perfected among the Brahmins. The jinn are worshipped only out of fear of their evil, and others out of desire for their mercy and fear of their wrath. Among them are the spouses, sons, and daughters of Allah.

These are the summaries of what Brahmanism contains and what the scholars of the creed among the Brahmins teach.

However, what is obtained from the *Upanishads* (١)—which is the fourth part of the sacred *Vedas*—may not agree with the aforementioned generalities of their beliefs, even if the scholars of the creed among the Brahmins interpret them.

For the critical researcher finds that the treatises of the *Upanishads* teaching divine knowledge (المعارف الإلهية), although they describe the divine world and the affairs related to it—such as the names, attributes, and acts of initiation, return, creation, sustenance, giving life, and causing death, and other things—using descriptions applied to physical, material matters—such as division, partitioning, rest, motion, transition, indwelling (الحلول), union (الاتحاد), greatness, smallness, and other physical, material states—they nevertheless explicitly state in several places that Brahm (٢) is an absolute essence (ذات مطلقة) transcendent beyond being encompassed by any limit. To him belong the Beautiful Names and the exalted attributes of life, knowledge, and power; he is transcendent beyond the descriptions of deficiency and the accidents of matter and body; there is nothing like him.

They explicitly state (٣) that He, the Exalted, is a single essence (إحدى الذات), was not born from anything, did not give birth to anything, and has no peer or likeness whatsoever.

They explicitly state (٤) that the truth is that none should be worshipped besides Him, the Exalted, nor should proximity be sought to any other through an offering; rather, the one worthy of worship is He alone, without any partner.

They frequently state (٥) the Resurrection (القيامة) and that it is the term to which creation ends. They describe the reward of deeds and their punishment after death in a way that does not refuse correspondence to the intermediate realm (البرزخ) without being necessarily interpreted as reincarnation.

There is no mention in these divine discussions presented therein of idols, statues, the directing of worship, or the presentation of offerings to them.

These which we have quoted from the *Upanishads*—and what we have left out is more—are noble truths and true knowledge (معارف حقة) in which the sound human primordial nature (الفطرة) finds tranquility. They—as you see—negate all the principles of idolatry presented at the beginning of the discussion.

What deep reflection guides to is that these were high truths discovered by individuals from the people of Allah's guardianship (أهل ولاية الله), who then informed some of their students who took from them of what they found. However, they mostly spoke in symbols (الرمز) and used parables (الأمثال) in their teachings.

Then, what was taken from these individuals was made the foundation upon which the way of life—which is the religion agreed upon by the general public—is built. These are subtle knowledges that none can bear except individuals from the people of knowledge (أهل المعرفة) because their level is higher than sense and imagination, which are the public's share of perception, and because of the extreme difficulty of perceiving them for ordinary intellects not trained in true knowledge.

Restricting their attainment to the few among people and depriving the majority of that is the first pitfall for a human religion. For the primordial nature (الفطرة) established the human world based on social gathering (الاجتماع المدني), and the separation of some from others in the way of life—which is religion—is an annulment of the law of the primordial nature and the method of creation.

Furthermore, in that is an abandonment of the path of the intellect (العقل), which is one of the three paths: Revelation (الوحي), Intuition (الكشف), and Intellect. It is the most general and important of them with regard to human worldly life. Revelation is not attained except by the people of infallibility (أهل العصمة) among the honored prophets; Intuition is not honored except for individuals from the people of sincerity and certainty; and people—even the people of Revelation and Intuition—are in dire need of employing intellectual proof (الحجة العقلية) in all affairs of worldly life and cannot do without it. In neglecting this path, compulsory imitation (التقليد الإجباري) is imposed upon all affairs of social life—beliefs, ethics, and actions—and in that is the fall of humanity.

Moreover, in that is the implementation of the law of enslavement (الاستعباد) in human society, as evidenced by long historical experience in human nations that lived in the religion of idolatry or in which the customs of enslavement were practiced by taking lords besides Allah.

## 2 - The Spread of These Pitfalls to Other Religions:

Other general religions, despite their profession of the monotheism of divinity (توحيد الألوهية), were not safe from the polytheism of worship (شرك العبادة). This led them to be afflicted with the very same pitfalls that Brahmanical idolatry was afflicted with, the most important of which are the three aforementioned.

As for Buddhism and the Sabians, this is manifest in them, and history bears witness to it. Something regarding their beliefs and actions has already been presented.

As for the Magians, they unify "Ahura Mazda" in divinity, but they submit in sanctification to Yazdan, Ahriman, the angels entrusted with the affairs of lordship, the sun, fire, and other things. History recounts the law of enslavement, class distinction, and management that was practiced among them. Reflection and consideration dictate that all of this only seeped into them from the direction of the distortion of the original religion. It has been narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding them: "They had a prophet whom they killed, and a book which they burned."

As for the Jews, the Quran recounts much of their actions, their distortion of the Book of Allah, their taking of scholars as lords besides Allah, and what Allah afflicted them with of the inversion of the primordial nature and the badness of disposition.

As for the Christians, we have detailed the discourse regarding what they deviated in of view and action in the third volume of the book, so refer to it. If you wish, apply the opening of the Gospel of John and the epistles of Paul to the other Gospels and complete it by reviewing the history of the Church; the discourse on that is long.

Deep research into all of that yields that the general calamities in religious societies in the human world are from the legacies of the first idolatry, which took divine knowledges and high true truths—unveiled and exposed—and made them the foundation of religious customs, imposing them upon the general understandings that only find familiarity with the sensible and the perceived. This produced what it produced.

## 3 - Islam's Reform of These Corruptions:

As for Islam, it reformed these corruptions by casting these high knowledges into the mold of simple expression (البيان الساذج) that is suitable for the digestion of simple understandings and ordinary intellects. Thus, they began to touch them from behind a veil and receive them wrapped and surrounded. This is what is suitable for the state of the general public. As for the elite (الخاصة), they attain them unveiled and exposed in their magnificent beauty and exquisite goodness, safe and tranquil, and they are in the company of those whom Allah has blessed among the prophets, the truthful, the martyrs, and the righteous, and excellent are those as companions. Allah the Exalted said: **وَالْكِتابِ الْمُبِينِ إِنَّا جَعَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتابِ لَدَيْنا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ** — "By the Manifest Book! We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason. And indeed it is in the Mother Book with Us, [and it is] surely sublime and wise" (43:2-4). And He said: **إِنَّهُ لَقُرْآنٌ كَرِيمٌ فِي كِتابٍ مَكْنُونٍ لا يَمَسُّهُ إِلَّا الْمُطَهَّرُونَ** — "This is indeed a noble Quran, in a protected Book—no one touches it except the pure" (56:77-79). And the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "We, the assembly of prophets, have been commanded to speak to people according to the capacity of their intellects."

It treated the disaster of polytheism and idolatry in the stage of monotheism by negating independence (الاستقلال) in essence and attributes from everything except Allah, the Glorified; for He, the Exalted, is the Sustainer (القيوم) over everything. It focused understandings in the knowledge of divinity between anthropomorphism (التشبيه) and transcendence (التنزيه), describing Him, the Exalted, as having life but not like our life, knowledge but not like our knowledge, power but not like our power, hearing but not like our hearing, and sight but not like our sight. In short, there is nothing like Him, and He is greater than can be described. Along with this, it commanded people not to say anything regarding that except based on knowledge, and not to lean toward a belief except based on an intellectual proof that their intellects and understandings can digest.

Thus, it succeeded, firstly, in presenting the religion to the general public and the elite as a single law; secondly, in allowing the sound intellect to function without leaving this divine gift in vain and unutilized; and thirdly, in bringing the different classes in human society as close as possible without blessing one and depriving another, or advancing one and delaying another. The Exalted said: **إِنَّ هذِهِ أُمَّتُكُمْ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً وَأَنَا رَبُّكُمْ فَاعْبُدُونِ** — "Indeed this community of yours is one community, and I am your Lord. So worship Me" (21:92). And He said: **يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّا خَلَقْناكُمْ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ وَأُنْثى وَجَعَلْناكُمْ شُعُوباً وَقَبائِلَ لِتَعارَفُوا إِنَّ أَكْرَمَكُمْ عِنْدَ اللهِ أَتْقاكُمْ** — "O mankind! Indeed We created you from a male and a female, and made you nations and tribes that you may identify yourselves with one another. Indeed the noblest of you in the sight of Allah is the most God-conscious among you" (49:13).

This is a summary of the discourse; you can find the detailed discourse on its aspects in various discussions that have preceded in this book, and Allah is the One whose help is sought.

4 - It may be thought that what is mentioned in supplications regarding seeking intercession (الاستشفاع) through the Prophet and his immaculate progeny (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), asking Him, the Exalted, by their right (بحقهم), visiting their graves, kissing them, seeking blessing from their soil, and venerating their traces is from the forbidden polytheism—which is idolatrous polytheism—arguing that this type of devotional orientation involves granting a lordly influence (تأثير ربوبي) to other than Him, the Exalted, which is polytheism. They argue that the practitioners of idolatry only associated partners because they said of their idols: "These are our intercessors with Allah," and their saying: "We only worship them so that they may bring us closer to Allah in rank." They claim there is no difference in worshipping other than Allah, the Glorified, whether that other is a prophet, a saint (ولي), a tyrant among tyrants, or others; all are from the forbidden polytheism.

What they have missed, firstly, is that the establishment of influence (التأثير)—whether material or non-material—in other than Him, the Exalted, is necessary and cannot be denied. He, the Exalted, has attributed influence of all kinds to other than Himself in His speech. Negating influence from other than Him, the Exalted, absolutely necessitates the nullification of the general law of cause and effect (العلية والمعلولية), which is the pillar in all proofs of monotheism, and in that is the destruction of the foundation of monotheism. Yes, what is negated of influence from other than Him, the Exalted, is independence (الاستقلال) in influence, and no one disputes this. As for negating absolute influence, in that is the denial of the self-evident truth of the intellect and a departure from the human primordial nature.

And whoever seeks intercession through the people of intercession (أهل الشفاعة) whom Allah mentioned in such as His saying: **وَلا يَمْلِكُ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ الشَّفاعَةَ إِلَّا مَنْ شَهدَ بِالْحَقِّ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ** — "Those whom they invoke besides Him do not possess [the power of] intercession, except those who bear witness to the truth and they know [it]" (43:86), and His saying: **وَلا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ارْتَضى** — "and they do not intercede except for him with whom He is pleased" (21:28).

Or asks Allah by their status (بجاههم) and adjures Him by their right (بحقهم) which He has granted them over Himself in such as His saying absolutely: **وَلَقَدْ سَبَقَتْ كَلِمَتُنا لِعِبادِنَا الْمُرْسَلِينَ إِنَّهُمْ لَهُمُ الْمَنْصُورُونَ وَإِنَّ جُنْدَنا لَهُمُ الْغالِبُونَ** — "Certainly Our decree has gone forth for Our servants, the apostles, that they will indeed be the ones to be helped, and that Our host will indeed be the victors" (37:171-173), and His saying: **إِنَّا لَنَنْصُرُ رُسُلَنا وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** — "Indeed We shall help Our apostles and those who have faith" (40:51).

Or venerates them and manifests love for them by visiting their graves, kissing them, and seeking blessing from their soil insofar as they are the signs (آيات) of Allah and His symbols (شعائره), holding fast to such as the saying of the Exalted: **وَمَنْ يُعَظِّمْ شَعائِرَ اللهِ فَإِنَّها مِنْ تَقْوَى الْقُلُوبِ** — "And whoever venerates the sacraments of Allah—indeed that arises from the God-consciousness of hearts" (22:32), and the verse of kinship (آية القربى) and other things from the Book and the Sunnah.

He is, in all of that, seeking the means (الوسيلة) through them to Allah, and He, the Exalted, has said: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللهَ وَابْتَغُوا إِلَيْهِ الْوسِيلَةَ** — "O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah, and seek the means [of recourse] to Him" (5:35). Thus, He legislated the seeking of the means through Him and made them—through what He legislated of loving them, honoring them, and venerating them—means to Him. There is no meaning to necessitating the love of something and its veneration while forbidding the traces of that. Therefore, there is no barrier to seeking proximity to Allah through loving them and venerating their affair and the traces of that, provided it is in the manner of recourse (التوسل) and seeking intercession (الاستشفاع) without granting them independence of influence or worship whatsoever.

Secondly, they have overlooked the distinction between worshipping other than Allah in the hope that they might intercede with Allah or bring one closer to Him, and worshipping Allah alone while seeking intercession (الاستشفاع) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — and proximity through them to Him. In the first case, independence (الاستقلال) is attributed to them and worship (العبادة) is devoted sincerely to other than the Exalted; this is polytheism (الشرك) in both servitude and worship. In the second case, independence is reserved solely for Allah, the Exalted, and worship is restricted to Him alone, without any partner.

Indeed, the Exalted only censured the polytheists (المشركين) for their saying: **مَا نَعْبُدُهُمْ إِلَّا لِيُقَرِّبُونَا إِلَى اللَّهِ زُلْفَىٰ** "...We only worship them so that they may bring us near to Allah." (39:3), because they granted them independence and directed their worship toward them instead of Allah, the Glorified. Had they said: "We worship Allah alone, yet we hope that His angels, His messengers, or His friends (أولياؤه) will intercede for us by His permission, or we seek a means (نتوسل) to Allah by honoring His symbols (شعائره) and loving His friends," they would not have disbelieved thereby. Rather, their "partners" would have become like the Kaaba (الكعبة) in Islam; it is a direction (وجهة) and not an object of worship, for it is Allah alone who is worshipped by turning toward it.

I wonder what these people would say regarding the Black Stone (الحجر الأسود) and what has been legislated in Islam concerning its touching and kissing? Likewise, what of the Kaaba? Is all of that a form of polytheism (الشرك) that has been exempted from the ruling of prohibition? The ruling [against polytheism] is a necessary rational judgment (حكم ضروري عقلي) that accepts neither specification (تخصصا) nor exception. Or is it that such acts are purely the worship of Allah, while the Stone serves as a path or a direction? If so, then what is the difference between it and anything else, provided its veneration is not done by attributing independence to it or devoting worship to it? The general proofs (مطلقات) for honoring the symbols of Allah (شعائر الله), venerating the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, loving him, and having affection for him and his Household (أهل بيته) remain valid in their respective places.
